authoringDefaults.description: Author fluid layouts by default; each parent defines the available space and its children declare how they fill, grow, shrink, wrap, and scroll within it.
rules: Derive compact, medium, expanded, orientation, and input snapshots from the chosen delivery targets; reflow instead of assigning a device-specific canvas.
Use parent-owned flex, grid, stack, and scroll constraints with intrinsic child sizing plus explicit fill, grow, shrink, min, and max behavior.
Keep a single scroll owner per axis and make overflow, wrapping, and paired anchors intentional.

mobile.description: Bare mobile requests use the stable flagship pair: SwiftUI on iOS and Jetpack Compose on Android.

defaultSelection.reason: This pair has the strongest native platform fit and stable release gates.

choices.label: Flagship native
choices.description: Separate SwiftUI and Jetpack Compose projects with native controls and platform conventions.
choices.bestFor: Production mobile delivery where native UX and stable release gates are the priority.
choices.tradeoffs: Maintains separate Apple and Android projects and toolchains.

choices.label: Shared Rust
choices.description: A shared Rust app/runtime delivered to both iOS and Android.
choices.bestFor: Rust teams that want shared systems logic across mobile platforms.
choices.tradeoffs: Beta lanes with platform signing and release matrices still required.

choices.label: Shared C#
choices.description: A shared .NET app/runtime delivered to both iOS and Android.
choices.bestFor: .NET teams that want shared application logic and tooling.
choices.tradeoffs: Beta lanes that require the mobile .NET workloads plus platform signing.

decisionQuestions: Only when the request suggests a single-OS scope: is delivery required for iOS, Android, or both? A bare mobile request means both.
Should the project use the flagship native pair, or does the team require a shared Rust or C# runtime?
Which simulator, device, signing, and store-account environments are available?

web.description: Bare web requests default to the authored vanilla HTML PWA and static build/hosting; static is a delivery mode and retains the applicable responsive and dynamic capability set. Ask for a framework lane and static versus SSR when that choice affects ownership or hosting.

defaultSelection.reason: Vanilla HTML is portable, installable, and requires no framework build or Node server.

choices.label: Vanilla HTML
choices.description: Semantic HTML, authored CSS, direct ES modules, clean routes, and an installable PWA without a build dependency.
choices.bestFor: Portable sites and apps with simple hosting and long-lived developer ownership.
choices.tradeoffs: Static build/hosting only but fully client-interactive; no framework component ecosystem or server rendering.

choices.label: React
choices.description: Strict TypeScript and React Router framework mode with functional routes and hooks.
choices.bestFor: Interactive products owned by React teams or dependent on the React ecosystem.
choices.tradeoffs: Adds a Node package/build toolchain; SSR requires a Node-compatible host.

choices.label: Vue
choices.description: A Nuxt project with file routes, Vue single-file components, script setup, and strict TypeScript.
choices.bestFor: Vue and Nuxt teams that want convention-led routing and universal rendering.
choices.tradeoffs: Adds a Node package/build toolchain; SSR requires a Node-compatible host.

choices.label: Angular
choices.description: A strict Angular workspace with standalone components, signals, typed services, and Angular Router.
choices.bestFor: Angular organizations that value an opinionated application and testing structure.
choices.tradeoffs: The largest framework/toolchain footprint; SSR requires a Node-compatible host.

choices.label: Astro
choices.description: HTML-first file routes and semantic components with client scripts or islands only where behavior needs them.
choices.bestFor: Content-rich and performance-sensitive experiences that benefit from selective hydration.
choices.tradeoffs: Interactive islands need explicit boundaries; SSR requires a Node-compatible host.

decisionQuestions: Which lane matches the team that will own the exported project?
Should the build/hosting mode be static or SSR? Vanilla HTML has no SSR mode, and static is the default for every framework lane; neither choice reduces responsive or dynamic behavior.
Which hosting provider, domain, Node runtime, environment variables, APIs, authentication cookies, offline policy, and cache boundaries apply?

desktop.description: Bare desktop requests default to cross-platform Rust; always present C# as the supported .NET alternative and macOS SwiftUI as an Apple-native alternative that requires a separately scoped/new exporter.

defaultSelection.reason: Rust is the default cross-platform desktop runtime for macOS, Linux, and Windows.

choices.label: Rust desktop
choices.description: A shared Rust-native desktop project for macOS, Linux, and Windows.
choices.bestFor: Cross-platform desktop products owned by Rust teams or requiring a small native runtime.
choices.tradeoffs: The desktop release matrix remains beta.

choices.label: C# desktop
choices.description: A shared .NET desktop project for macOS, Linux, and Windows.
choices.bestFor: .NET teams that want shared desktop application logic and tooling.
choices.tradeoffs: The desktop release matrix remains beta and requires the .NET toolchain.

unavailableChoices.label: SwiftUI desktop
unavailableChoices.description: The Apple-native macOS desktop alternative, suited to teams that require SwiftUI and platform-specific macOS conventions. NativeUI does not currently provide this target; ios-swiftui produces iOS/iPadOS mobile output only.
unavailableChoices.bestFor: A macOS-only product whose owners explicitly prefer Apple-native SwiftUI over a cross-platform runtime.
unavailableChoices.tradeoffs: Requires a separately scoped/new macos-swiftui exporter and macOS-specific build, signing, and notarization work before NativeUI can produce it.
unavailableChoices.followUp: Always present this alternative with its unavailable status, then ask whether the user intends to scope the new macOS-only exporter; do not present ios-swiftui as desktop output.

decisionQuestions: Which desktop operating systems and packaging formats are required?
After seeing all three choices, does the owning team prefer the default Rust lane, the available C# lane, or a newly scoped macOS-only SwiftUI exporter?
If macOS SwiftUI is chosen, is the user explicitly authorizing the separately scoped exporter work? NativeUI does not currently register that target.

targets.description: Native iOS/iPadOS flagship output using SwiftUI and developer-owned async app actions.
targets.bestFor: Default Apple mobile delivery where native UX and stable release gates matter.
targets.tradeoffs: Apple-only output that requires Xcode and Apple signing.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS with NativeUI responsive, interaction, and timeline vocabulary

targets.description: Legacy native iOS output using UIKit and NativeUI reflow semantics.
targets.bestFor: Existing UIKit integrations that explicitly require the legacy lane.
targets.tradeoffs: Beta and opt-in; prefer SwiftUI for new flagship iOS work.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS; select the nativeui-reflow lane explicitly

targets.description: Native Android flagship output using Jetpack Compose and developer-owned suspend app actions.
targets.bestFor: Default Android delivery where native UX and stable release gates matter.
targets.tradeoffs: Android-only output that requires the Java, Gradle, and Android SDK toolchain.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS with NativeUI responsive, interaction, and timeline vocabulary

targets.description: Legacy native Android output using Views/XML and NativeUI layout semantics.
targets.bestFor: Existing Android Views integrations that explicitly require the legacy lane.
targets.tradeoffs: Beta and opt-in; prefer Compose for new flagship Android work.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS; select the nativeui-views lane explicitly

targets.description: A shared Rust-native desktop project for macOS, Linux, and Windows.
targets.bestFor: Cross-platform desktop products owned by Rust teams or requiring a small native runtime.
targets.tradeoffs: The cross-platform desktop release matrix remains beta.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS; shared Rust stage registry

targets.description: Native iOS delivery backed by the shared NativeUI Rust/Cocoa runtime.
targets.bestFor: Rust teams sharing systems and application logic across mobile targets.
targets.tradeoffs: Beta; still requires Xcode, Cocoa integration, and Apple signing.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS; shared Rust stage registry

targets.description: Native Android delivery backed by the shared NativeUI Rust Android runtime.
targets.bestFor: Rust teams sharing systems and application logic across mobile targets.
targets.tradeoffs: Beta; still requires the Android NDK, SDK, and signing workflow.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS; shared Rust stage registry

targets.description: Browser delivery from the shared NativeUI Rust runtime compiled to WebAssembly.
targets.bestFor: Rust-heavy applications that need a browser target from the shared runtime.
targets.tradeoffs: Beta and runtime-oriented; use an authored web lane when developer-owned web source is the priority.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS; shared Rust stage registry compiled to WASM

targets.description: An authored semantic HTML, plain CSS, and direct ES-module PWA with clean routes and no build dependency.
targets.bestFor: Portable CDN-hosted sites and interactive PWAs with simple hosting and long-lived developer ownership.
targets.tradeoffs: Static build/hosting only but fully client-interactive; no framework component ecosystem or server rendering.
targets.authoringSyntax: Semantic HTML, plain CSS, and direct ES-module behavior

targets.description: A strict TypeScript React Router framework-mode PWA with functional routes and hooks.
targets.bestFor: Interactive products owned by React teams or dependent on the React ecosystem.
targets.tradeoffs: Adds a Node package/build toolchain; SSR requires a Node-compatible host.
targets.authoringSyntax: Strict TypeScript, functional route components, hooks, and literal Tailwind classes

targets.description: A Nuxt PWA with file routes, Vue single-file components, script setup, and strict TypeScript.
targets.bestFor: Vue and Nuxt teams that want convention-led routing and universal rendering.
targets.tradeoffs: Adds a Node package/build toolchain; SSR requires a Node-compatible host.
targets.authoringSyntax: Strict TypeScript, file routes, Vue SFCs, and script setup

targets.description: A strict Angular PWA with standalone components, signals, typed services, and Angular Router.
targets.bestFor: Angular organizations that value an opinionated application and testing structure.
targets.tradeoffs: The largest framework/toolchain footprint; SSR requires a Node-compatible host.
targets.authoringSyntax: Strict Angular workspace, standalone components, signals, router, and templates

targets.description: An HTML-first Astro PWA with file routes and client scripts or islands only where behavior requires them.
targets.bestFor: Content-rich and performance-sensitive experiences that benefit from selective hydration.
targets.tradeoffs: Interactive islands need explicit boundaries; SSR requires a Node-compatible host.
targets.authoringSyntax: Strict Astro file routes and client scripts only where behavior requires them

targets.description: A shared .NET desktop project for macOS, Linux, and Windows.
targets.bestFor: Cross-platform desktop products owned by .NET teams.
targets.tradeoffs: The desktop release matrix remains beta and requires the .NET toolchain.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS; shared generated C# stage registry

targets.description: Native iOS delivery backed by the shared NativeUI .NET runtime.
targets.bestFor: .NET teams sharing application logic across mobile targets.
targets.tradeoffs: Beta; requires the .NET iOS workload, Xcode, and Apple signing.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS; shared generated C# stage registry
knownGates: .NET iOS workload/signing release matrix remains beta

targets.description: Native Android delivery backed by the shared NativeUI .NET runtime.
targets.bestFor: .NET teams sharing application logic across mobile targets.
targets.tradeoffs: Beta; requires the .NET Android workload, SDK, and signing workflow.
targets.authoringSyntax: Plain HTML/CSS; shared generated C# stage registry
knownGates: .NET Android workload/signing release matrix remains beta
